notes.anchor: in ruins of the commonwealth and of mine
notes.note: Cicero dates his earlier silence to the collapse of the republican order in the years of his own exile (58-57 BC), which he routinely figures as a shared catastrophe (ruinae rei publicae). The phrase yokes Sittius's private ruin to the state's, and to Cicero's own.

notes.anchor: the unpopularity over the corn supply
notes.note: Invidia annonae: the public anger over grain shortages and high prices at Rome in 57-56 BC, which inflamed the courts and (Cicero argues) helped doom the younger Sittius's case. The corn supply (annona) was a chronic flashpoint of late-republican politics.

notes.anchor: neither in work, nor counsel, nor labour, nor influence, nor testimony
notes.note: A formal catalogue of the services (officia) a Roman patron rendered a friend's son on trial: practical help, legal advice, exertion, the weight of his standing (gratia), and sworn character-testimony. Such lists were the currency of aristocratic friendship.

notes.anchor: that you are both a man and a citizen
notes.note: Hominem te et virum esse: the Stoic-tinged commonplace of consolation that a man should bear the blows of fortune as befits a rational being and a member of the community. Cicero deploys it as the hinge of the letter's exhortation.
